input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring permanent magnet synchronous motors and gear transmission components, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides active components for real-time data interpretation and processing to enable bidirectional communication with the physical system, contrasting with the passive one-way data flow of a digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for system modeling; methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for complexity management; architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector architecture; MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavior modeling; integration with MathWorks Simulink, additional simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow operates with real-time data-driven representations, continuously updating the digital model from live data flows originating from the physical system via a one-way communication channel as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Concrete Digital Shadow Model (Fig. 5) comprises Source components for raw data trace collection, Asset components representing the physical system, and Processing components that execute computational steps using simulation or data calculation models. Traceability is enforced via 'originates from' associations, which link output data traces to their generating processing components and consumed input traces, ensuring transitive traceability across the system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling (including component interfaces and interconnections via Internal Block Diagrams), behavioral modeling through state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, and analytical models via MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code (C++/Java). Data sources consist of physical system state traces, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, managed through integration of heterogeneous modeling tools and methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time-series data from physical sensors, including input current and output angle, are streamed via MQTT protocol to the digital shadow system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between physical sensors and digital components is established via MQTT protocol using Mosquitto as the server implementation and Thingsboard for IoT data management.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the specific robot model, axis count, motor type, transmission components, and controller unit as detailed in Section III of the source text.

The extraction correctly captures the conceptual distinction between a digital shadow and a digital twin's role regarding data flow, as described in Section II.B. The text states a digital shadow has 'an automated one-way data flow' and is 'passive', whereas software systems 'require active components to interpret and process this incoming data', which a 'Digital Twin (DT) can provide'. The extraction accurately reflects this contrast and the service of active data interpretation.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by Section II.A. The text lists 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses... SysML', methodologies such as 'RFLP... OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT', architecture description languages like 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs', behavior modeling via 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML', and integration with 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. The extraction provides a comprehensive technical list of these tools.

or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately captures the temporal aspects described in the text. It highlights 'real-time data-driven representations' and 'continuously updating' via a one-way communication channel. The source text confirms this by defining the digital shadow as 'real-time, data-driven representations' and characterizing it by 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'. The description is specific and well-grounded, though it could be expanded with more detail on specific latency or frequencies mentioned (e.g., 'live measurements').

The document focuses on a single digital shadow instance ('FrictionShadow') for one axis of a robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures between twins, or centralized versus decentralized architectures.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the Concrete Digital Shadow Model, accurately listing Source, Asset, and Processing components. It correctly describes traceability via 'originates from' associations. The source text fully supports this, stating: 'A digital shadow consists of several data series... Source components for raw data trace collection, Asset components representing the physical system, and Processing components that execute computational steps' and 'By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component'. The extraction is comprehensive and well-grounded.

The document describes the integration of the digital shadow with the physical robot (vertical integration) using MQTT and AAS, but does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, or cross-system interoperability standards as defined by horizontal integration.
